_cite.reason: item_type correction: route item to its true surface by generated-brief substance (Technology folded per operator Option B 2026-06-07)

prior.title: New Brunswick Environment and Local Government Department - Climate Action and Water Strategy Resources
prior.summary: New Brunswick's Environment and Local Government department landing page providing access to climate change initiatives, water management strategies, and local governance reforms. Key resources include the Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, Water Strategy 2018-2028, Output-Based Pricing System for large emitters, and various environmental monitoring tools.
prior.full_brief:
# New Brunswick Environment and Local Government — Climate Action and Water Strategy Resources

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

New Brunswick's provincial climate and water governance framework has advanced across three interlocking tracks in the 2022–2025 period, each generating direct operational signals for freight-intensive industries operating in or through the province.

**Track 1 — GHG Reduction Trajectory.** The province's Climate Change Action Plan (CCAP) Progress Report 2024–2025 documents a 42% emission reduction since 2005. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, Government of New Brunswick, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html

The legislated 2030 target is 10.7 Mt CO₂e. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, Government of New Brunswick, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html The ICAP registry records a separate, more ambitious CCAP target of a 46% reduction below 2005 levels by 2030 and net-zero by 2050. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the 10.7 Mt figure and the 46%-below-2005 figure represent the same legislated commitment expressed in different units, or represent two distinct targets with separate enforcement mechanisms, requires legal and regulatory confirmation against the Climate Change Act text.

**Track 2 — Output-Based Pricing System (OBPS).** The NB OBPS has been in force since January 2021, covering large industrial emitters in the power, industry, and mining-and-extractives sectors. The set price for the 2025 compliance year is CAD 95 per tCO₂e, rising to CAD 110 for the 2026 compliance year, and following a trajectory to CAD 170 per tCO₂e in 2030. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

**Track 3 — Water Strategy.** A Water Strategy for New Brunswick 2018–2028 is the active ten-year framework governing provincial water resource management. A 2021 Progress Report and a 2024 Five-Year Review have been published. *Source: GNB – A Water Strategy for New Brunswick 2018–2028, Government of New Brunswick. https://www2.gnb.ca/content/gnb/en/departments/elg/environment/content/water/content/water_strategy.html

The trigger for elevated attention to all three tracks is the convergence of the carbon price escalation schedule with the CCAP's 2025–2027 action deadlines — specifically actions targeting zero-emission freight and fuel switching — and the midpoint review of the Water Strategy, which is generating stakeholder pressure on unresolved climate-water integration gaps.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Carbon pricing and OBPS administration** is driven by the Government of New Brunswick (Department of Environment and Local Government / Climate Change Secretariat), operating under the Climate Change Act and the Reduction of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Regulation. The provincial system was established to demonstrate equivalence with the federal OBPS and thereby retain provincial control of carbon pricing for large industrial emitters. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

As of 2024, 15 facilities participate in the NB OBPS, including seven voluntary opt-in participants. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

**The CCAP 2022–2027** commits the province to specific action timelines on zero-emission freight, fuel infrastructure, and building performance that cut across supply chain operations. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php

**The Water Strategy** is a ten-year government framework. Its 2024 Five-Year Review has attracted NGO scrutiny. The Conservation Council of New Brunswick, in its February 2024 assessment of that review, flagged gaps in Indigenous voice integration and climate change action linkages. *Source: Conservation Council of NB – Water Strategy Progress Report Assessment, February 2024. https://www.conservationcouncil.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/CCNB-Comments-on-Water-Strategy-Progress-Report-and-Review-February-2024.pdf

Analytical inference: The NGO critique signals that the Water Strategy's second half (2024–2028) is likely to face pressure for stronger climate-water integration language, which could tighten operational permissions around water use and discharge at industrial and logistics sites in the province.

**CCAP Indigenous engagement** is a stated new action: the plan commits to establishing a working group with Indigenous communities and organizations by 2024 to identify climate change priority areas and facilitate information sharing. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**OBPS carbon price trajectory (sourced, binding schedule):**
The NB OBPS price follows the federal trajectory, rising CAD 15 per year until 2030, reaching CAD 170 per tCO₂e. The 2026 compliance price is CAD 110. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

*Specific forward annual price steps between 2026 and 2030 not individually itemised in the source blocks as of this date; the trajectory formula (CAD 15/year increase from 2026 base of CAD 110 to 2030 cap of CAD 170) is derivable from the sourced ICAP fact sheet but the intermediate annual figures are an* Analytical inference.

**CCAP action deadlines converting signals to operational pressure:**

The CCAP identifies the following dated commitments relevant to freight and logistics operations, each of which converts a signal into a compliance or procurement event if implemented on schedule:

- By 2025: Investigate and, if appropriate, set a minimum proportion of RNG and/or clean hydrogen for fuel supply. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2025: Develop a Clean Electricity Strategy for achieving net-zero electricity emissions by 2035. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2025: Conduct a Clean Technology Skills Gap Assessment. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2025: 6% of recent light-duty vehicle sales to be electric vehicles. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2026: A drought index and public advisory reporting system. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2026: Implement a Living Shorelines program. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2026: Train 300 new and existing tradespeople in skills related to residential and commercial high-performance buildings. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2027: Support development of renewable methane projects. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2030: Phase out heating oil use in all buildings. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2030: 50% of recent light-duty vehicle sales to be electric vehicles. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2030: Strategic implementation of RNG and clean hydrogen re-fueling infrastructure, prioritizing co-location with existing fueling stations. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php
- By 2030: Net-zero electricity emissions target. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php

The CCAP also commits to supporting the transition to zero-emission freight transportation as a specific new action, without a fixed delivery date in the sourced content. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php

*Specific implementation regulations or enforcement instruments arising from these CCAP commitments not identified in the source blocks as of this date.* Analytical inference: the 2025 and 2026 milestone dates have now passed or are imminent; whether corresponding regulations or standards were formally adopted on schedule is a research gap requiring direct confirmation from the Climate Change Secretariat or the 2024–2025 Progress Report (PDF not ingested in source blocks).

**Re-check window:** Re-examine this brief in Q3 2025 when the 2024–2025 CCAP Progress Report content (referenced in the GNB action page) can be fully reviewed for milestone delivery status, and again in Q1 2026 for the 2026 compliance price step.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**Filtered by transport mode (road primary for NB provincial operations; air and ocean tertiary):**

**Carbon cost escalation — road freight operating in New Brunswick:**

Analytical inference: Workspaces operating road freight corridors through New Brunswick that also operate or sub-contract to facilities meeting the 50,000 tCO₂e threshold face direct OBPS compliance costs at the escalating carbon price (CAD 95 in 2025, CAD 110 in 2026, trajectory to CAD 170 in 2030). For workspaces below the 50,000 tCO₂e threshold but above 10,000 tCO₂e, voluntary opt-in to the OBPS is available, which may provide access to the OBPS Industry Fund for GHG reduction project financing. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Output-Based Pricing System, Government of New Brunswick. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html; ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

**OBPS Industry Fund — access to green project capital:**

In 2023, the government developed and implemented a New Brunswick OBPS Industry Fund, as an additional stream of the New Brunswick Climate Change Fund, which returns proceeds collected from fund credit transactions to NB OBPS participants to support GHG emission reduction projects, with all proceeds allocated via a merit-based process. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system The list of funded projects for 2025 and 2026, as of May 2025, includes 56 projects led by 12 different departments or organizations for a total value of CAD 58.4 million. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

Analytical inference: For workspaces that are OBPS-registered participants (mandatory or opt-in), the OBPS Industry Fund is a live capital channel for fleet electrification, RNG infrastructure, or facility efficiency projects — the merit-based process means early, well-structured applications are more competitive.

**Fuel transition cost — heating oil phase-out:**

The CCAP commits to working with the federal government toward the phase-out of heating oil use in all buildings by 2030, including identifying transition support for heating oil delivery companies. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php

Operational implication: Workspaces with New Brunswick warehouse, depot, or gateway facilities heated by oil face a conversion cost obligation before 2030. Transition support programs are referenced but their scope, eligibility, and quantum are not specified in the sourced content as of this date.

**MRV compliance overhead — reporting and verification:**

Facilities registered under the NB OBPS face an annual reporting cycle: Verified Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reports are due June 1 of the year following the compliance period, and Compliance Reports are due December 15 of the year following the compliance period. Reports must be verified by a team accredited to ISO Standard 14065. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Output-Based Pricing System, Government of New Brunswick. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html; ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

Analytical inference: For workspaces without existing verified emissions reporting infrastructure in New Brunswick, the cost of establishing accredited third-party verification is an additional overhead that should be scoped before voluntary opt-in decisions are made.

**GHG reporting standard update — 2025:**

New Brunswick published a new version of "The Reporting and Reduction of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standard" in May 2025, containing a new section on the reporting of renewable natural gas for the purpose of calculating total regulated emissions; an expanded definition of accredited verification bodies; and the requirement for entities to use IPCC Fifth Assessment Report Global Warming Potential values starting from their 2025 emissions data. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system

Operational implication: Workspaces already reporting under the NB OBPS must apply IPCC AR5 GWP values from 2025 data onward — this is a methodological change that could affect reported emission totals for facilities using fuels other than CO₂-dominant sources, particularly those with methane-intensive operations.

**Water Strategy — operational implications for NB-based facilities:**

The Water Strategy 2018–2028 governs water resource management across the province. The 2024 Five-Year Review is complete, and the strategy runs to 2028. *Source: GNB – A Water Strategy for New Brunswick 2018–2028, Government of New Brunswick. https://www2.gnb.ca/content/gnb/en/departments/elg/environment/content/water/content/water_strategy.html

*Specific operational requirements or permit conditions arising from the Water Strategy for freight, logistics, or warehousing operations are not identified in the source blocks as of this date.* Analytical inference: Facilities drawing on New Brunswick surface or groundwater for cooling, washing, or industrial processes should monitor the second-half (2024–2028) implementation of the Water Strategy, particularly any tightening of watershed management conditions that may flow from the 2024 review and NGO pressure noted above.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*Named competitor activity in New Brunswick's freight and logistics sector relative to NB OBPS participation or CCAP programme engagement is not identified in the source blocks as of this date.*

Analytical inference: The NB OBPS Industry Fund's merit-based allocation means early-mover operators who structure OBPS-eligible GHG reduction projects (fleet electrification, RNG adoption, efficiency upgrades) and submit funding applications ahead of annual cycles gain access to capital that is closed to later applicants in the same round. Workspaces that delay voluntary opt-in decisions lose eligibility for this funding stream for that compliance year.

Analytical inference: The CCAP's zero-emission freight transition action, though undated in the sourced content, signals that NB could introduce freight-specific emission requirements or procurement preferences in government contracting. Operators able to demonstrate low-carbon fleet capacity in the province will be better positioned for public-sector freight contracts as those requirements harden.

The 2024–2025 CCAP Progress Report references stronger collaboration with Indigenous communities as a key achievement alongside the 42% emission reduction. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, Government of New Brunswick, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html Analytical inference: Freight operators with demonstrated Indigenous engagement frameworks will be differentiated in provincial tender processes where Indigenous collaboration is an assessed criterion.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- New Brunswick has achieved a 42% reduction in GHG emissions since 2005 as of the 2024–2025 Progress Report, ahead of the legislated 10.7 Mt target for 2030. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, Government of New Brunswick, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html This is a factual, sourced provincial performance figure that can be cited in client sustainability discussions about the NB corridor.
- The NB carbon price for large industrial emitters is CAD 95/tCO₂e in 2025 and rises to CAD 170/tCO₂e by 2030, following the federal trajectory. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system
- The province's CCAP includes a specific commitment to support the transition to zero-emission freight transportation. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php This means clients routing high-value or time-sensitive freight through NB can reference a provincial policy alignment with ZEF.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert that the CCAP's action deadlines have been met without consulting the 2024–2025 Progress Report directly (PDF not ingested). Several 2025 milestones are listed in the plan; their delivery status is a research gap.
- Do not characterise the OBPS as covering road freight operators generally — it applies to point-source industrial and power facilities above 50,000 tCO₂e, not to freight carriers as a class.
- Do not cite the Conservation Council's 2024 critique of the Water Strategy as a government finding — it is an NGO stakeholder assessment, not authoritative guidance.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Do your New Brunswick supplier or customer facilities operate above the 50,000 tCO₂e threshold? If so, their OBPS compliance costs are rising and may be passed through in production or handling costs.
- Are your NB-based operations heated by oil? The 2030 heating oil phase-out commitment creates a transition cost that should be in the capital planning horizon now.
- Does your ESG reporting framework require disclosure of Scope 3 emissions from freight corridors through NB? The province's carbon price trajectory gives clients a quantifiable cost signal for decarbonisation planning.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Map NB facility emissions against OBPS thresholds.**
For any facility in the workspace's NB footprint (owned, leased, or sub-contracted), establish whether it emits above 50,000 tCO₂e (mandatory OBPS) or between 10,000 and 50,000 tCO₂e (voluntary opt-in eligible). *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Output-Based Pricing System, Government of New Brunswick. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html This mapping is the prerequisite for all other OBPS decisions.

**Action 2 — Evaluate voluntary opt-in against OBPS Industry Fund access.**
Facilities between 10,000 and 50,000 tCO₂e should model whether voluntary opt-in delivers net benefit through access to the OBPS Industry Fund for GHG reduction capital, against the compliance overhead of verified reporting. The merit-based fund had 56 projects worth CAD 58.4 million committed for 2025–2026. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system Voluntary opt-in forms are due December 1 of the year before the opted-in designation is meant to take effect. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Output-Based Pricing System, Government of New Brunswick. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html

**Action 3 — Update GHG reporting methodology to IPCC AR5 GWP values for 2025 data.**
The May 2025 update to the Reporting and Reduction of GHG Emissions Standard introduces this requirement for 2025 emissions data. *Source: ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System, International Carbon Action Partnership, 2025. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system Workspaces currently reporting under the NB OBPS should confirm their monitoring and reporting systems are updated before the June 2026 emissions report deadline.

**Action 4 — Begin capital planning for heating oil phase-out at NB facilities.**
The CCAP targets elimination of heating oil in all buildings by 2030. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php Workspaces with NB depots, warehouses, or gateway facilities on oil heat have fewer than five years to complete conversion. Transition support programs are referenced in the CCAP but their specific terms are not published in the source blocks.

**Action 5 — Monitor CCAP zero-emission freight action for regulatory instrument.**
The CCAP commitment to support the transition to zero-emission freight transportation does not carry a fixed date in the sourced content. *Source: UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027, University of New Brunswick, December 2022. https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php The workspace should set a watch on the Climate Change Secretariat's annual fund project lists and regulatory calendar for any instrument translating this commitment into a procurement standard or operator requirement.

**Action 6 — Review the 2024–2025 CCAP Progress Report (PDF).**
The GNB action page references a 2024–2025 Progress Report (PDF 6.2 MB) as a published document. *Source: GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change, Government of New Brunswick, 2025. https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html This document was not ingested in the source blocks for this brief. It should be retrieved and reviewed for milestone delivery status against the 2025 deadlines listed in the CCAP — specifically the Clean Electricity Strategy, the Clean Technology Skills Gap Assessment, and the RNG/hydrogen proportion investigation.

---

# 8. Sources

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------------|------------------------|
| GNB – New Brunswick's Action on Climate Change | https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/climate-change-action.html | 2 | Official GNB programme page; primary source for the 42% GHG reduction figure and 10.7 Mt 2030 target; essential for tracking annual CCAP progress reports |
| GNB – New Brunswick's Output-Based Pricing System | https://www.gnb.ca/en/topic/environment-resources/climate-change/output-based-pricing-system.html | 2 | Official GNB OBPS page; authoritative source for mandatory/opt-in thresholds, reporting deadlines, and registration procedures |
| GNB – A Water Strategy for New Brunswick 2018–2028 | https://www2.gnb.ca/content/gnb/en/departments/elg/environment/content/water/content/water_strategy.html | 2 | Official strategy landing page linking to 2021 Progress Report and 2024 Five-Year Review; essential for monitoring the strategy's second-half implementation |
| ICAP – Canada: New Brunswick Output-Based Pricing System | https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/canada-new-brunswick-output-based-pricing-system | 3 | Authoritative international carbon-market registry; provides price trajectory, OBPS Industry Fund detail, MRV design, and penalty structure not consolidated on the GNB page |
| UNB Sustainability – Summary of NB Climate Change Action Plan 2022-2027 | https://blogs.unb.ca/thegreenreview/2022/12/unb-sustainability-nb-climate-change-action-plan-summary.php | 6 | Academic summary of CCAP 2022-2027; provides structured pillar-by-pillar action listing with dated commitments; source for the CCAP's specific freight and fuel transition actions |
| Conservation Council of NB – Water Strategy Progress Report Assessment | https://www.conservationcouncil.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/CCNB-Comments-on-Water-Strategy-Progress-Report-and-Review-February-2024.pdf | 6 | NGO critique of the 2024 Five-Year Review; signals where civil society pressure may drive tighter climate-water integration in the strategy's second half; not authoritative but a leading indicator |
